items.prompt_md: What is the purpose of the `dummy` node in the merge?
options: It holds the final answer directly, so `dummy` itself is returned
It gives `tail` a valid node to point from before any real node has been attached, avoiding a "first node" special case
It stores the total length of the merged list
It marks the boundary between the two input lists
items.answer: It gives `tail` a valid node to point from before any real node has been attached, avoiding a "first node" special case
items.explanation_md: `dummy.next` is always where the real merged list begins. `tail` starts at `dummy`, so the very first attachment is written through `tail.next` exactly like every later one.

items.prompt_md: Why does the function return `dummy.next` instead of `dummy` itself?
options: Because `dummy` is a placeholder node that was never meant to be part of the real answer — the actual merged list starts one node after it
Because `dummy.next` is always `None`
Because returning `dummy` would raise an exception
Because `dummy` and `dummy.next` are always the same node
items.answer: Because `dummy` is a placeholder node that was never meant to be part of the real answer — the actual merged list starts one node after it
items.explanation_md: The sentinel exists purely to simplify the loop's bookkeeping; it's discarded at the end, and only the chain of real nodes attached after it is returned.

items.prompt_md: What bug would appear if the code forgot the final line `tail.next = l1 or l2` after the main merge loop ends?
options: Nothing — one of the input lists is always empty by that point
Whichever list still has remaining nodes would be silently dropped from the result, since the loop only runs while both l1 and l2 are non-empty
It would cause an infinite loop
It would merge the lists in the wrong order
items.answer: Whichever list still has remaining nodes would be silently dropped from the result, since the loop only runs while both l1 and l2 are non-empty
items.explanation_md: The `while l1 and l2` loop exits as soon as either list is exhausted, so the leftover tail of the other list must be explicitly spliced on — skipping this truncates the result.

items.prompt_md: Why is it safe to splice on the remainder with `tail.next = l1 or l2` rather than walking the leftover list node by node?
options: Because whichever list still has nodes left is already sorted internally, so attaching its head links in the rest of it correctly for free
Because the leftover list is always empty
Because `l1` and `l2` are guaranteed to have the same length
Because Python automatically merges any remaining nodes
items.answer: Because whichever list still has nodes left is already sorted internally, so attaching its head links in the rest of it correctly for free
items.explanation_md: Since both inputs were sorted to begin with, once one is exhausted, every remaining node in the other is already in correct relative order — no further comparisons are needed.

items.prompt_md: Without a dummy head, what extra logic would the merge loop need to handle correctly?
options: A branch that checks whether this is the very first node being attached, so it can set the real `head` variable instead of writing through `tail.next`
A branch that checks whether either input list is sorted
A branch that reverses one of the two lists first
Nothing — the loop would work identically without a dummy head
items.answer: A branch that checks whether this is the very first node being attached, so it can set the real `head` variable instead of writing through `tail.next`
items.explanation_md: Without a sentinel, there's no valid `tail` to write through before the first real node exists, forcing an `if head is None: head = node else: tail.next = node` split that the dummy head eliminates entirely.

items.prompt_md: What is the risk if the merge loop's comparison uses `l1.val < l2.val` (strictly less than) instead of `l1.val <= l2.val` when the two current values are equal?
options: It's still correct — equal values are attached in a different order (l2's node before l1's, instead of l1's before l2's), but the result is still fully sorted and stable behavior can differ only in relative order of equal elements
It causes an infinite loop
It silently drops one of the equal-valued nodes
It always produces an unsorted result
items.answer: It's still correct — equal values are attached in a different order (l2's node before l1's, instead of l1's before l2's), but the result is still fully sorted and stable behavior can differ only in relative order of equal elements
items.explanation_md: Both `<=` and `<` produce a fully sorted merged list; the only difference is which of two equal-valued nodes (from l1 vs l2) ends up first, not correctness of the sort order.

items.prompt_md: Why does the naive `if head is None: head = node else: tail.next = node` branch inside a merge loop count as "repeated logic for a one-time event"?
options: Because the `if head is None` check is only ever true once (for the very first node attached), yet it's evaluated on every single iteration of the loop
Because it runs the loop twice
Because it duplicates the entire merge logic in two separate functions
Because it needs two separate loops instead of one
items.answer: Because the `if head is None` check is only ever true once (for the very first node attached), yet it's evaluated on every single iteration of the loop
items.explanation_md: The condition can only fire on the first attachment, but without a dummy head, every iteration still has to pay the cost of checking it — a dummy head removes the check entirely rather than just making it cheap.

items.prompt_md: How does a dummy head generalize beyond merging two sorted lists — for example, to removing a node from a singly linked list?
options: It doesn't apply outside of merging sorted lists
Whenever the node that ends up first in the result could change (e.g., removing the actual head node), a sentinel before it means the "is this the head?" case never needs separate handling
A dummy head only works when there are exactly two input lists
Removing a node never needs a dummy head, only merging does
items.answer: Whenever the node that ends up first in the result could change (e.g., removing the actual head node), a sentinel before it means the "is this the head?" case never needs separate handling
items.explanation_md: Any operation where the true head might be removed or replaced benefits from the same trick — a sentinel node makes "what if the first node changes" a non-issue, since the sentinel's `next` always tracks it.

items.prompt_md: What edge case does the merge handle correctly if one of the two input lists is empty (`None`) from the very start?
options: The `while l1 and l2` loop never executes, and `tail.next = l1 or l2` immediately attaches the entire non-empty list — the empty list contributes nothing
It raises an exception because one list has no nodes
It returns `None` regardless of the other list's contents
It requires a special `if l1 is None or l2 is None` branch before the loop starts
items.answer: The `while l1 and l2` loop never executes, and `tail.next = l1 or l2` immediately attaches the entire non-empty list — the empty list contributes nothing
items.explanation_md: The loop guard is false immediately when either list is `None`, so the whole merge degenerates gracefully into just splicing on the other (possibly also empty) list — no extra branch required.

items.prompt_md: Why is `tail = tail.next` a necessary line inside the merge loop, distinct from `tail.next, l1 = l1, l1.next`?
options: Because `tail.next, l1 = l1, l1.next` only rewires which node comes after `tail` — `tail` itself must then be advanced to that newly attached node so the next attachment goes after it, not the same spot again
It's redundant and could be safely deleted
It only matters on the very last iteration
It resets `tail` back to `dummy` for the next round
items.answer: Because `tail.next, l1 = l1, l1.next` only rewires which node comes after `tail` — `tail` itself must then be advanced to that newly attached node so the next attachment goes after it, not the same spot again
items.explanation_md: Without advancing `tail`, every subsequent attachment would overwrite `tail.next` instead of extending the chain, destroying everything attached so far except the very last write.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of merging two sorted linked lists of total length n with a dummy head?
options: O(n)
O(n log n)
items.answer: O(n)
items.explanation_md: Each of the n nodes across both lists is visited and relinked exactly once during the merge, plus a constant amount of leftover splicing — O(n) total.

items.prompt_md: What is the extra space complexity of the dummy-head merge, not counting the two input lists?
options: O(log n)
O(n)
items.explanation_md: Only the `dummy` sentinel node and the `tail` pointer are extra; existing nodes from both input lists are reused and relinked, not copied.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of merging k sorted linked lists, of total length n across all of them, by repeatedly scanning all k current heads to find the smallest each step?
options: O(n)
O(n log k)
O(n * k)
items.answer: O(n * k)
items.explanation_md: Each of the n total nodes requires an O(k) scan across all k current heads to find the minimum, giving O(n * k) — using a heap of size k instead would cut this to O(n log k).

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of merging two sorted linked lists by copying both into an array, sorting the array, and rebuilding a list, for total length n?
options: O(n)
O(n log n)
O(log n)
items.answer: O(n log n)
items.explanation_md: Sorting the combined array costs O(n log n), which is strictly worse than the O(n) the dummy-head merge achieves by exploiting the fact both inputs are already sorted.

items.prompt_md: After the main merge loop ends, splicing the remaining tail of whichever list still has nodes (`tail.next = l1 or l2`) costs what time complexity?
options: O(n)
O(n log n)
items.explanation_md: This is a single pointer assignment — attaching the head of the remaining list — not a node-by-node walk, so it's constant time regardless of how many nodes remain.

items.prompt_md: You're merging two already-sorted singly linked lists into one sorted list by repeatedly taking the smaller head. Which approach fits without special-casing the first node attached?
options: Dummy head + merge
Fast & slow pointers
Reverse pointers in place
Copy both lists into an array, sort, and rebuild
items.answer: Dummy head + merge
items.explanation_md: A dummy sentinel before the real head means `tail.next` is always a valid write target, even for the very first node attached — no branch needed.

items.prompt_md: You must remove the node right after a given node from a singly linked list, and you want the code to work the same way even if the node to remove happens to be the current head. Which approach fits?
options: Dummy head placed before the real head, so removal logic never special-cases the head
Fast & slow pointers
Sort the list first
Copy the list into an array
items.answer: Dummy head placed before the real head, so removal logic never special-cases the head
items.explanation_md: Whenever the true head of the result might change (here, by removal), a sentinel node in front absorbs that special case, just like it does for the first node of a merge.

items.prompt_md: You're partitioning a singly linked list around a value x, building two separate chains (nodes less than x, nodes greater or equal) before splicing them together, and want to avoid a "first node of each chain" special case. Which approach fits?
options: A dummy head for each of the two chains being built, with a trailing tail pointer for each
Fast & slow pointers
Copy nodes to an array first
Hash set of visited nodes
items.answer: A dummy head for each of the two chains being built, with a trailing tail pointer for each
items.explanation_md: Building any new chain by repeated attachment benefits from the same trick — a sentinel per chain removes the "is this the first node of this chain" branch.

items.prompt_md: You're given two sorted ARRAYS (not linked lists) and must merge them into one sorted array in O(n + m) time. Which approach fits an array under this constraint?
options: Two pointers, one per array, always copying whichever points at the smaller current element into the output
Dummy head + merge
Fast & slow pointers
Reverse pointers in place
items.answer: Two pointers, one per array, always copying whichever points at the smaller current element into the output
items.explanation_md: Trap: merging sorted sequences sounds like the dummy-head merge, but arrays have indices and random access rather than `next` pointers to relink — the array two-pointer merge walk is the native tool here.

items.prompt_md: Given an array of meeting time intervals, you must merge all overlapping intervals into their union. Which approach fits?
options: Sort intervals by start time, then sweep once, merging the current interval into the last one whenever they overlap
Dummy head + merge
Fast & slow pointers
Hash set of visited nodes
items.answer: Sort intervals by start time, then sweep once, merging the current interval into the last one whenever they overlap
items.explanation_md: Trap: "merge" cues the dummy-head linked-list pattern, but this is an array of interval objects with no `next` pointers — sorting plus a single sweep is the correct array-based approach.

items.prompt_md: You need to merge two dictionaries (hash maps) of word counts into one combined count, summing counts for words that appear in both. Which approach fits?
options: Iterate one dictionary's key-value pairs and add each count into the other dictionary, creating new keys as needed
Dummy head + merge
Fast & slow pointers
Reverse pointers in place
items.answer: Iterate one dictionary's key-value pairs and add each count into the other dictionary, creating new keys as needed
items.explanation_md: Trap: "merge" again suggests the linked-list dummy-head trick, but combining two hash maps has nothing to do with `next` pointers — it's a hash-map key-by-key accumulation.

items.prompt_md: You must merge k sorted linked lists into a single sorted list, picking the smallest of the k current heads at each step using a min-heap for efficiency. Which approach fits as the underlying list-building mechanism, on top of the heap for selecting the minimum?
options: Dummy head + merge, popping the smallest head from the heap and re-pushing its next node each step
Fast & slow pointers
Copy all k lists into one array and sort it
Reverse pointers in place
items.answer: Dummy head + merge, popping the smallest head from the heap and re-pushing its next node each step
items.explanation_md: Extending the two-list dummy-head merge to k lists still needs the sentinel-and-trailing-tail mechanism to build the output chain; the heap only changes how the next-smallest node is selected each step.

items.prompt_md: You're inserting a new node into the correct sorted position of an already-sorted singly linked list, and the new node might become the new head. Which approach fits so the insertion logic doesn't need a special case for "inserting before the current head"?
options: Dummy head placed before the real head, walking a trailing pointer to find the insertion point
Fast & slow pointers
Copy the list to an array, insert, and rebuild
Hash set of visited nodes
items.answer: Dummy head placed before the real head, walking a trailing pointer to find the insertion point
items.explanation_md: Since the new node could end up before the current head, a sentinel node removes the need to special-case that possibility — insertion before the sentinel's successor is handled the same way as any other insertion.

items.prompt_md: You have two sorted arrays and need to find the median of their combined elements without fully merging them, in better than O(n + m) time. Which approach fits?
options: Binary search on the smaller array's partition point, using index arithmetic to balance the two halves
Dummy head + merge
Fast & slow pointers
Reverse pointers in place
items.answer: Binary search on the smaller array's partition point, using index arithmetic to balance the two halves
items.explanation_md: Trap: two sorted sequences again evokes the dummy-head merge, but needing better than linear time on arrays (which support binary search via indices) calls for a partition-based binary search, not a linked-list splice.

items.prompt_md: You must add two numbers represented as singly linked lists in reverse digit order (each node one digit), producing a new linked list for the sum, handling carries and differing lengths gracefully. Which approach fits for building the result list without a first-node special case?
options: Dummy head + running tail pointer, appending one sum-digit node per step while tracking carry
Fast & slow pointers
Reverse pointers in place
Copy both lists into arrays and add them as big integers
items.answer: Dummy head + running tail pointer, appending one sum-digit node per step while tracking carry
items.explanation_md: Building any new list by repeated appends — digit sums included — benefits from the sentinel-plus-trailing-tail trick, so the very first digit node is attached with the same code path as every later one.
